message:
fix(cli,server): tolerate orphaned project metadata + harden mail status / projects adopt / TUI compose paths

Five focused robustness fixes that all share the same theme as the
recent reconstruct/repair commits: prefer surfacing structured
errors and falling back to archive/synthetic data over silently
returning zero or crashing when project metadata is partially
missing or malformed.

crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/lib.rs

(1) New helper load_project_alias_doc_for_update(path) ->
    CliResult<serde_json::Value>:
    - Returns an empty json object when the path doesn't exist.
    - Reads + parses + validates that the result is a JSON object.
    - On read or parse failure, returns CliError::Other naming
      the path and the underlying error.
    - On a non-object root, returns CliError::Other naming the
      path and demanding a JSON object.
    Replaces the open-coded
        if path.exists() { read_to_string -> parse -> unwrap_or_default }
        else { json!({}) }
    sequence inside handle_projects_adopt_with_conn() that
    silently swallowed parse errors and returned an empty doc,
    which meant a malformed aliases.json could be overwritten by
    a half-built one without anyone noticing.
    The new helper is called BEFORE the database mutation begins,
    so a malformed aliases.json fails closed: the projects-adopt
    command exits with a clear error and leaves both the source
    project's mail data and the target archive untouched.

(2) handle_projects_adopt_with_conn() reorder + atomicity:
    - Move the alias-doc load to the top of the function (before
      any conn.query_sync() that mutates state).  Prior order
      meant we'd already deleted source product links by the time
      we noticed the aliases.json was malformed; the cleanup was
      then half-done and the operator had to recover by hand.
    - Now: first load and validate aliases.json; only then run
      the duplicate-agent check, the mailbox-busy check, and
      finally the destructive cleanup.

(3) handle_mail_status_sync(): tolerate missing project metadata.
    - Resolve the project once via crate::context::resolve_project
      and treat InvalidArgument as "no DB row, fall back to
      archive scan" instead of erroring out.
    - When the DB row exists, count messages/agents via
      WHERE project_id = ? (not the JOIN-on-slug variant), which
      is both faster and safer when slugs change.
    - When the DB row is missing, call a new helper
      archive_mail_status_counts(storage_root, slug) that walks
      the archive at storage_root/projects/<slug>/messages/ and
      storage_root/projects/<slug>/agents/ and returns
      Some((message_count, agent_count)) if the directories
      exist, or None.  This recovers a meaningful "Project: X /
      Messages: N / Agents: M" output for projects whose DB row
      was wiped by an earlier broken cleanup but whose archive
      survived.
    - On both paths missing, fall back to (slug, 0, 0) so the
      command still prints the project header instead of
      panicking.

(4) parse_product_inbox_attachments_json(attachments_json: &str)
    -> Vec<serde_json::Value>:
    Replace the previous
        serde_json::from_str(&r.message.attachments).unwrap_or_default()
    inside product_inbox_row_to_json() with a helper that:
    - Returns Vec::new() for empty input.
    - Returns the parsed array for valid JSON.
    - Returns a single-element marker
        [{ "name": "[malformed-attachments-json]",
           "media_type": null, "path": null, "bytes": null }]
      when parsing fails on non-empty input.
    The marker preserves a signal in the JSON output that the
    on-disk row is malformed, instead of silently dropping the
    attachments list.  Downstream consumers that scan for
    "[malformed-attachments-json]" can flag rows for repair.

(5) New tests:
    - product_inbox_row_to_json_preserves_malformed_attachment_signal:
      builds an InboxRow with attachments = "{not-json}", calls
      product_inbox_row_to_json(&row, false), and asserts the
      attachments array has one entry with
      name == "[malformed-attachments-json]".
    - integration_mail_status_uses_archive_counts_when_project_metadata_is_missing:
      seeds a DB+archive, deletes the projects row, and asserts
      `mail status` still prints the right project slug, message
      count, and agent count from the archive scan.
    - integration_projects_adopt_apply_rejects_malformed_aliases_before_mutation:
      seeds a clean DB plus a target archive with a malformed
      aliases.json, runs handle_projects_adopt_with_conn(),
      asserts the call returns CliError::Other with "parse" and
      "aliases.json" in the message, and verifies that:
        * source agent count is unchanged;
        * target agent count is unchanged;
        * source archive m1.md is still in place;
        * target archive messages/m1.md was never created;
        * malformed aliases.json was not rewritten.

(6) Fixture cleanup: integration_doctor_repair_preserves_messages_when_project_metadata_is_missing
    now also seeds an OrphanSender agent row with id=1 instead of
    leaving sender_id NULL on the orphan-project message, so the
    fixture matches what the doctor repair preservation test
    actually exercises.

crates/mcp-agent-mail-server/src/tui_screens/messages.rs

- TUI compose / live-mutation paths previously did
    SELECT a.name FROM agents a JOIN projects p ON p.id = a.project_id WHERE p.slug = ?
  to resolve the agents-for-compose list and
    SELECT id FROM projects WHERE slug = ? LIMIT 1
  to resolve the project id for live mutations.  Both fail when
  the user is operating on a synthetic-project-id slug
  (the "[unknown-project-N]" placeholder used when the projects
  row is missing) because the projects table doesn't have a row
  to JOIN against.
- Switch both call sites to:
    if let Some(project_id) = synthetic_project_id(project_slug) {
        … query directly by project_id …
    } else {
        … existing slug → id resolution …
    }
- The compose-agents query becomes
    SELECT name FROM agents WHERE project_id = ? ORDER BY name
  for the synthetic case and
    SELECT a.name FROM agents a
        WHERE a.project_id = (SELECT id FROM projects WHERE slug = ? LIMIT 1)
        ORDER BY a.name
  for the slug case (subquery instead of JOIN so it returns an
  empty result rather than failing when the projects row is
  missing entirely).
- Two new TUI integration tests assert:
    * Composing into a synthetic-project-id slug enumerates the
      orphaned agents from the agents table.
    * Live mutation against a synthetic-project-id slug uses the
      derived project_id and writes the new message row instead
      of failing with "Project not found".

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
